title: Potential Automorphy for Compatible Systems of l-Adic Galois Representations
speaker: David Geraghty
affiliation: Princeton University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: I will describe a joint work with Barnet-Lamb, Gee and Taylor where we establish a potential automorphy result for compatible systems of Galois representations over totally real and CM fields. This is deduced from a potential automorphy result for single l-adic Galois representations satisfying a `diagonalizability' condition at the places dividing l.

proof_stages.label: Automorphic Galois representations and their self-duality
proof_stages.summary: For a regular algebraic conjugate self-dual automorphic representation pi, the talk recalls the associated semisimple l-adic Galois representations, their local-global compatibility, distinct Hodge–Tate weights, and the symmetric pairing expressing total odd conjugate self-duality.

proof_stages.label: Weak and strict compatible systems
proof_stages.summary: A weakly compatible system is defined by Galois representations with common Frobenius characteristic polynomials away from bad primes and common Hodge–Tate weights, with strict compatibility upgrading this to Weil–Deligne compatibility at every prime.

proof_stages.label: Potential automorphy theorems A and B
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem for compatible systems states that every irreducible regular totally odd conjugate self-dual weakly compatible system becomes automorphic after a finite CM Galois extension, and a parallel single-representation theorem with residual irreducibility and potential diagonalizability is stated as the input that implies it.

proof_stages.label: L-functions, purity, and compatible-system consequences
proof_stages.summary: Potential automorphy is used to deduce strict compatibility, strict purity of weight n-1, convergence, meromorphic continuation and functional equation of the associated L-function, and that a single representation satisfying theorem B lies in a strictly compatible pure system.

proof_stages.label: Extreme regularity and density-one irreducibility
proof_stages.summary: For automorphic systems with extremely regular Hodge–Tate weights, Rankin–Selberg pole-order comparisons combined with potential automorphy of irreducible constituents force r_lambda(pi) to be irreducible for a Dirichlet-density-one set of rational primes.

proof_stages.label: Potential diagonalizability as the local condition at l
proof_stages.summary: Potential diagonalizability is defined by lying, after a finite local extension, on the same crystalline deformation-ring component as a diagonal representation, and the talk records ordinary/upper-triangular and Fontaine–Laffaille criteria that make the condition usable.

proof_stages.label: Residual potential automorphy via auxiliary geometry
proof_stages.summary: The first proof step for theorem B constructs an auxiliary finite scheme from a family of smooth projective varieties and congruences to an automorphic induction, then finds a CM Galois point and applies modularity lifting to obtain an ordinary automorphic lift of the residual representation.

proof_stages.label: Modularity lifting and local component matching
proof_stages.summary: The second proof step is a modularity lifting theorem: after arranging local component agreement away from l by Khare–Wintenberger-type lifting and at l by tensoring with induced characters chosen to give diagonal local pieces, the Taylor–Wiles–Kisin method proves the original representation automorphic.

examples.name: Sato–Tate as an application of compatible systems
examples.note: The speaker mentions the proof of the Sato–Tate conjecture as an application illustrating why placing a single Galois representation into a pure compatible system is useful, without deriving any new idea from the example here.
examples.engagement_reason: Sato–Tate is only alluded to as a conjectural application of the compatible-system result, with no computation or construction carried out on it.

examples.name: The set {0,1,2,3} is not extremely regular
examples.note: The subset-sum collision 0+3=1+2 makes the abstract extreme-regularity condition concrete; it illustrates the definition rather than generating the later irreducibility argument.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker verifies non-extreme-regularity for {0,1,2,3} by exhibiting the equal subset sums 0+3=1+2.

examples.name: Base schemes such as P^1 or P^1 minus points
examples.note: The speaker says one may think of the base of the auxiliary family of varieties as P^1 or P^1 with points removed, merely to visualize the geometric construction used in residual potential automorphy.
examples.engagement_reason: P^1 and P^1 minus points are named only as possible base schemes for the general family construction, while the subsequent argument is carried out for an arbitrary base B.

passes.reason: Projective-line-type bases are given as illustrative choices, with P^1 itself a maximally symmetric genus-zero curve.
